claims.claim: A 12-column CSS grid with grid-column/grid-row span properties (not Flexbox) is the recommended foundation for bento layouts, because Flexbox is one-dimensional and cannot handle the two-dimensional spanning required.
claims.quote: a 12-column grid gives maximum flexibility. The core code pattern uses grid-column: span X and grid-row: span X rather than Flexbox, which is one-dimensional.

claims.claim: Bento tile sizing should follow a four-tier hierarchy: hero tiles (4–6 col × 2 row), feature tiles (3–4 col × 1–2 row), metric cards (2–3 col × 1 row), and accent tiles (1–2 col × 1 row) — directly mapping visual size to data importance.
claims.quote: Tile size must reflect data importance, not data volume. Oversized tiles for minor metrics waste space; undersized tiles for key metrics create hierarchy confusion.

claims.claim: Having three or more hero-sized tiles in a single grid destroys visual anchoring, defeating the hierarchy benefit of the bento pattern over a uniform card layout.
claims.quote: Three+ hero tiles eliminate visual anchoring.

claims.claim: A gap of 16px is the recommended default between tiles; below 8px tiles visually merge and above 32px the grid loses cohesion.
claims.quote: Recommend 16px gap; below 8px tiles merge; above 32px loses cohesion.

claims.claim: Users form dashboard impressions in under six seconds, and bento grids solve the 'data density problem' by using Gestalt principles of proximity and similarity to pre-answer where focus should go, unlike uniform card layouts where every element competes equally.
claims.quote: Users form dashboard impressions in under six seconds. Bento grids address 'data density problem' by using Gestalt principles—proximity and similarity—to guide visual scanning. Unlike card layouts where every element competes for the same amount of attention, bento sizing pre-answers where users should focus.
